title: Community sanitation corner
summary: A low-tech place for handwashing, waste separation, cleaning tools, and illness prevention when normal services fail.
content: Sanitation collapses quietly: dirty hands near food, mixed waste, flies, bad smells, blocked drains, and children playing near contaminated water. A sanitation corner is a marked place that holds washing water, soap or clean ash when soap is absent, trash bags, cleaning tools, sharp-waste separation, and simple written rules. It must be far from drinking water and food preparation but close enough that people actually use it. The aim is routine, not perfection. Daily small cleaning prevents disease better than rare deep cleaning after the place is already disgusting.
materials: Gloves if available. Context: “Community sanitation corner”.
Tongs or cardboard for sharp waste
steps: Place the station away from drinking water, food, sleeping, and play areas.
Post handwashing rules before food, after toilet use, after waste handling, and after caring for sick people.
Separate food scraps, human waste, greywater, sharp items, and medical waste.
Mark cleaning tools so they are not used for food or water containers.
Inspect daily for smell, flies, leaks, full bags, dirty water, and blocked paths.
warnings: Human waste and dirty water spread through shoes and hands before people notice.
Do not shame people publicly; correct the system and repeat rules.
Do not mix sharp objects with normal trash.
common_mistakes: Putting the station too far away while carrying out “Community sanitation corner”
Using the same cloth for toilet, floor, table, and water containers.
Treating flies and smell as normal.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Community sanitation corner with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Community sanitation corner if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Community sanitation corner and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Community sanitation corner or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Community sanitation corner are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Community sanitation corner”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Community sanitation corner as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Community sanitation corner when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Community sanitation corner.
short_term: For the first hours of Community sanitation corner, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Community sanitation corner remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Community sanitation corner fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Community sanitation corner to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
